local_names.romanized_local: Za-lone Htamin (ဇလုံထမင်း)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Burmese Zalone rice bowl; closest parent 'rice dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as category anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Burmese Zalone rice bowl; A000E (rice-based mixed dish) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Burmese rice dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g) with mixed protein, vegetable accompaniments, and sauce (approximately 200 g combined).

nutrient_source.title: Cooked white rice and mixed Burmese-style curry accompaniments — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice, stir-fried vegetables, and chicken curry components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Zalone Burmese Rice Bowl exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by component-weighted averaging: cooked white rice (USDA FDC #168878, ~50% by weight), stir-fried mixed vegetables (~20%), chicken curry (~25%), and condiments/sauce (~5%). Macros represent a moderate-protein, moderate-fat mixed rice bowl typical of Burmese cuisine.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed rice dish analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Zalone Burmese Rice Bowl. GI estimated at 64 based on the analogous food 'boiled white rice mixed dish' from the University of Sydney GI Database (white rice GI ~72 attenuated by protein, fat, and vegetable accompaniments to approximately 64, consistent with published mixed rice dish GI values of 60–68). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (64 × 82 g) / 100 ≈ 52.5, where available carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 20.5 × 4 = 82 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce, soy sauce, and curry seasonings
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving provides approximately 1280 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce portions or substitute low-sodium soy sauce and limit serving frequency to once weekly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving (~52.5) from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI and high glycemic load of this dish can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion to 100–150 g, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitoring blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from curry sauce and protein components combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of approximately 4.4 g saturated fat and 1280 mg sodium per serving warrants portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing lean protein options (tofu or skinless chicken) and requesting reduced oil preparation can improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zalone Rice Set (Burmese Rice Bowl), known in Burmese as ဇလုံထမင်း (Za-lone Htamin), is a traditional Burmese-style mixed rice bowl dish served as a complete meal set. It consists of a generous portion of steamed white jasmine rice topped or accompanied by a combination of protein (typically chicken, pork, or tofu), stir-fried or braised mixed vegetables, and a flavourful Burmese curry or stir-fry sauce seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, fried shallots, garlic, turmeric, and sesame oil. The dish is characterised by its savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile with aromatic notes from turmeric and shallots, and a satisfying combination of soft rice with tender protein and lightly cooked vegetables. In Singapore, this dish is served at Burmese and Southeast Asian casual dining establishments and represents an accessible, filling lunch or dinner option. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving provides approximately 592 kcal, 27 g protein, 17 g fat, and 82 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, protein-adequate meal. The sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning, which is a clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving are relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, who should consider portion reduction of the rice component. The dish is dairy-free and can be adapted to be vegetarian by substituting tofu for meat protein.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Zalone Burmese Rice Bowl was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice, stir-fried vegetables, and chicken curry. GI is estimated from analogous mixed white rice dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was listed as 'Italian' — this is clearly erroneous given the food name, Burmese script, and restaurant context. Cuisine has been corrected to 'Burmese'. 2. The input cuisine_tags (Italian, Pizza, Coffee, Toast) reflect the restaurant's broader menu at The Light Cafe - High Street Plaza and do not describe this specific dish; they have been disregarded for this record. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component sources used for estimation. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero based on the plant-oil and lean-protein composition of the dish with no industrial partially hydrogenated fats expected. 5. Halal status is listed as false because the dish may contain pork depending on restaurant preparation; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant. 6. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this dish has not been published. 7. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate based on typical Burmese rice bowl set portions; actual restaurant portion may vary. 8. Sodium estimate of 320 mg per 100 g reflects typical Burmese curry seasoning levels; actual sodium may be higher depending on sauce quantity used.
